_meta.issue: #171 Rebuild ścieżek — bank pytań P2 „Bezpieczne używanie AI”, moduł MB6 (weryfikacja treści AI)
_meta.dataPolicy: WSZYSTKIE dane SYNTETYCZNE (przyklad.test, NotkaApp/SklepDemo, Jan Syntetyczny, identyfikatory testowe). ZERO realnych danych/PII.
_meta.note: Pytania modułu MB6 (Q147-Q152), pula dedykowana ścieżki P2. Bez pytań krytycznych. Plik <800 LOC (AGENTS).

questions.learningOutcome: The participant understands why the confident tone of an AI answer says nothing about its truthfulness.
questions.prompt: Why can an AI answer sound very confident and still be wrong?

options.text: The model generates the most linguistically probable answer — fluency and a confident tone are not a measure of truth.
Models lie on purpose so that the user comes back and asks more questions.
A confident tone only appears when the model is connected to the internet.
That is impossible — if an answer sounds confident, the model has sources for it.

questions.feedbackCorrect: The model predicts the next words; it does not check facts — that is why it can deliver truth and fabrication (a hallucination) with the same flair. The tone of an answer is style, not proof.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: The model does not lie on purpose and does not signal certainty with sources — it generates the most linguistically probable answer, so an error sounds just as confident as the truth (A). Review the section on the nature of hallucinations.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant verifies a source provided by AI instead of trusting the mere fact that it was cited.
questions.prompt: AI prepared a paragraph for the NotkaApp company newsletter with a specific statistic and a link to a report. It sounds professional. What do you do before publishing?

options.text: I publish — since the AI provided a link, the statistic is confirmed.
I open the link and check whether the report exists and actually contains this statistic; I also look for a second, independent confirmation.
I remove the link and keep just the statistic — without a link nobody will check and there will be no problem.
I ask the same AI to confirm that the statistic is true — if it confirms, I publish.

questions.feedbackCorrect: Yes: models can make up both the number and a credible-looking link. Verification = open the source, check the content, and find independent confirmation — only then publish.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: A link by itself proves nothing (it can be made up), hiding the link does not fix a wrong number, and “confirmation” from the same model is the same unreliable machine. Correct: open and check the source plus a second independent trace (B).

questions.learningOutcome: The participant recognizes elevated-risk topics where an AI answer requires verification by a specialist.
questions.prompt: A colleague wants to send his mom medication dosage recommendations generated by AI, “because they sound sensible and are nicely laid out”. What do you advise him?

options.text: He should send them — AI was trained on medical texts, so it knows better than many a doctor.
He should send them, but with a note that they come from AI — mom will decide for herself whether to trust them.

Medication dosing is an elevated-risk topic: the recommendations must be confirmed by a doctor or pharmacist, and the generated text can be a starting point for a conversation with them, not a recommendation.
He should ask a second AI model and compare — if the answers match, he can send them.

questions.feedbackCorrect: Yes: health, law, and money are the topics where an AI error costs the most — here the verifier is a specialist, not a second model. AI can help prepare questions for the doctor, not replace their decision.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: In high-risk topics (health, law, finance), agreement between two models or a “from AI” note is not enough — both models can repeat the same error, and you cannot shift the responsibility onto mom. Recommendations are confirmed by a doctor or pharmacist (C).

questions.learningOutcome: The participant recognizes the limits of the model’s knowledge (data freshness) as a source of errors.
questions.prompt: Scenario: you ask AI about the current tax relief regulations. The model answers in detail and matter-of-factly, but with no dates or sources. Which risk is the MOST important here?

options.text: The answer may describe the legal situation from months or years ago — the model’s knowledge has a cutoff date, and regulations change.
The answer is too detailed — shorter answers are always more reliable.
The model may have answered in the wrong dialect of the language.
No risk — tax regulations are stable enough that the model’s knowledge date does not matter.

questions.feedbackCorrect: The most serious risk is staleness: the model “stopped” at the end date of its training data, and the law changes often. Always verify “current regulations/rates/deadlines” topics in an official, dated source.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: Answer length and style are not measures of risk — what matters is the model’s knowledge cutoff date: the legal situation described may be out of date (A). Check regulations in an official, dated source.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant builds the habit of verification proportional to the weight of the decision before passing content on.
questions.prompt: You have 10 minutes before the SklepDemo board meeting. AI summarized a 40-page report for you, and based on the summary you are to recommend a budget decision. What do you do?

options.text: I present the summary in full — with so little time there is no other way, and AI is rarely wrong.
I verify in the original report the key numbers and conclusions the recommendation rests on; I mark the rest of the summary as unverified and say so openly.
I cancel my part of the meeting — without reading all 40 pages, nothing may be recommended.
I ask the AI for a second, shorter summary and compare the two — agreement between summaries confirms correctness.

questions.feedbackCorrect: Yes: verification proportional to the stakes — what carries the decision (numbers, conclusions) you check in the source; the rest you label openly. Transparency with the board is part of integrity, and two summaries from the same model are not independent.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: Presenting an unverified summary for a budget decision passes a potential hallucination on to the board; two summaries from the same machine are not independent verification; canceling the meeting is escape, not risk management. Correct: verify the key parts in the original and openly label the rest (B).

questions.learningOutcome: The participant knows the basic steps of quickly verifying AI-generated content.
questions.prompt: Which steps belong to a quick verification of an AI answer before using it further? Select all that apply.

options.text: Checking whether the cited source exists and actually contains the referenced information.
Assessing whether the answer sounds confident and professional.
Looking for a second, independent confirmation of the key facts and numbers.
Checking whether the answer is long enough and nicely formatted.

questions.feedbackCorrect: Exactly: verification rests on sources (whether they exist and confirm the content) and an independent trace for the key facts. Tone, length, and formatting are cosmetics — they say nothing about truthfulness.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: A confident tone, length, and nice formatting are style features the model generates just as readily for truth and for fabrication. What verifies are sources (A) and independent confirmation (C).
